original_abstract: Stimuli-responsive hydrogels capable of programmable shape transformations are vital components for the advancement of soft robotics, 4D printing, and intelligent biomedical systems. Despite their potential, these technologies increasingly demand hydrogel architectures that are not only structurally complex and heterogeneous, but also modular and reconfigurable. In this work, we demonstrate a method for constructing such advanced hydrogel assemblies by reversibly bonding discrete units using a light-controlled metallopolymer adhesive. This adhesive facilitates robust interfacial attachment through a combination of metal–ligand coordination and polymer chain entanglement. By integrating units that respond to temperature and pH, we developed assemblies capable of sophisticated, multi-stimuli-responsive motions and shape-shifting behaviors. Uniquely, these structures can be disassembled under light irradiation and subsequently reassembled or reconfigured via thermal treatment, allowing for the flexible reprogramming of their functional properties. The use of reversible adhesives to build and remodel hydrogel systems offers a versatile framework for designing next-generation intelligent materials and soft robots with customizable, user-defined capabilities.
humanized_abstract: Stimuli-responsive hydrogels that can change shape in a pre-programmed way are a key component of soft robotics, 4D printing and intelligent biomedical systems. As these applications grow, so too does the need for more complex and heterogeneous hydrogel structures and, crucially, modular and reconfigurable ones. Here we report that discrete hydrogel building blocks can be reversibly “glued” and “unstuck” in a light controlled manner by using a metallopolymer adhesive that allows strong, permanent bond and, upon activation by light of a different wavelength, releases the hydrogel building blocks through disruption of the metal ligand coordination bonds accompanied by breaking of polymer entanglements. The glued and unstuck hydrogel building blocks consisted of either temperature responsive or pH responsive units, and were demonstrated to exhibit multifaceted movements and reversible shape change upon actuation by multiple stimuli. Moreover, the hydrogel structure could be disassembled under blue light irradiation, and reassembled and reconfigured under a thermal treatment, allowing the material properties to be re-programmed. In summary, the reversible adhesion method presented here provides a general principle for constructing and rearranging hydrogel systems for advanced materials and potential soft robotic applications with on-demand characteristics.
